coredata.dc:title: An abiotic carbon dots@ ZIF-90 fluorescent probe for rapid and reliable detection of adenosine triphosphate
coredata.dc:creator: Xin, Yue
Zhang, Dun
Zeng, Yan
Qi, Peng
coredata.dc:description: ATP is a high-energy compound that plays a vital role in biological metabolism. Abnormal changes in ATP concentration are related to various diseases and reflect microbial metabolism in biofilms. In this work, we prepared carbon quantum dots (CDs) with aggregation-induced fluorescence inhibition effect using the bacterial culture medium as raw material with a hydrothermal method. Then, an abiotic fluorescent nanoprobe named CDs@zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-90 (ZIF-90) was facilely synthesized by encapsulating CDs into ZIF-90. Owing to the encapsulation of CDs in the hollow structure of ZIF-90, the blue fluorescence emission of CDs@ZIF-90 decreased significantly. In the presence of ATP, the ZIF-90 framework was destroyed due to the strong coordination between ATP and Zn2+. The released CDs exhibited stronger fluorescence intensity, which was closely related to the ATP concentration. The convenient synthesis process and rapid ATP-responsive ability make CDs@ZIF-90 highly promising for clinical and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adenosine triphosphate
Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks
Carbon dots
Abiotic fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of ATP with CDs@ZIF-90
Fluorescence assay of ATP in real samples
Feasibility of ATP detection based on abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe
ATP detection performances of the abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images (the inset shows the diameter distribution of CDs) (A), C 1s XPS spectrum (B) of CDs.

TEM image of CDs@ZIF-90 (A), the XRD patterns of the simulated ZIF-90, synthesized pure ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 (B), the FT-IR spectra of synthesized CDs, ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 (C), the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, ZIF-90, CDs@ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 after the addition of ATP (D), The concentrations of CDs, ZIF-90, CDs@ZIF-90 and ATP were 0.062 g L−1, 1 g L−1, 1 g L−1 and 50 μM, respectively.
TEM image of CDs@ZIF-90 after addition of ATP (A), and the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-90 before and after addition of ATP (B).

Fluorescence spectra of the CDs@ZIF-90 nanoprobe after addition of ATP with different concentrations (A), calibration plots between the changes of fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 nanoprobe and ATP concentration (B), the changes of fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 nanoprobe towards 20 μM ATP, 20 μM AMP, 20 μM ADP, and 20 μM CTP (C), and the fluorescence emission of CDs@ZIF-90 within 12 days storage and the corresponding changes towards 20 μM ATP (D).
The ATP concentrations within Desulfovibrio sp biofilms on glass and stainless-steel substrates.
Schematic of the synthesis of the CDs@ZIF-90 composites for ATP sensing.
Comparison with other methods for ATP detection.
ATP detection in serum samples using the proposed method and ATP detection kit.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: represents the results using the proposed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: represents the results using the ATP detection kit.

head.ce:title: An abiotic carbon dots@ ZIF-90 fluorescent probe for rapid and reliable detection of adenosine triphosphate

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Corrosion and Bio-fouling

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ATP is a high-energy compound that plays a vital role in biological metabolism. Abnormal changes in ATP concentration are related to various diseases and reflect microbial metabolism in biofilms. In this work, we prepared carbon quantum dots (CDs) with aggregation-induced fluorescence inhibition effect using the bacterial culture medium as raw material with a hydrothermal method. Then, an abiotic fluorescent nanoprobe named CDs@zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-90 (ZIF-90) was facilely synthesized by encapsulating CDs into ZIF-90. Owing to the encapsulation of CDs in the hollow structure of ZIF-90, the blue fluorescence emission of CDs@ZIF-90 decreased significantly. In the presence of ATP, the ZIF-90 framework was destroyed due to the strong coordination between ATP and Zn2+. The released CDs exhibited stronger fluorescence intensity, which was closely related to the ATP concentration. The convenient synthesis process and rapid ATP-responsive ability make CDs@ZIF-90 highly promising for clinical and environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Being a universal ‘high energy’ compound, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) plays an important role as the energy currency in living cells [1]. It is indispensable for processes such as cell metabolism, enzyme catalysis, material transportation, energy transformation, cell growth, and apoptosis; changes in ATP concentration can be used to characterize cellular activity. For instance, clinical studies have reported that abnormal ATP concentrations are associated with tumors, cardiovascular diseases, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease [2]. In addition, since the formation of microbial biofilms is often accompanied by changes in ATP concentrations, detecting extracellular ATP within biofilms is important in diagnosing bacterial infection and microbial corrosion [3].

Antibodies and aptamers are the most commonly used biological recognition materials for ATP [4,5]. However, biological materials are expensive and unstable, and selective binding is easily affected by nonspecific absorption. In recent years, researchers have focused on developing fluorescent probes for ATP detection because fluorescent probes have the advantages of low cost, easy operation, high sensitivity, fast result output, and visualization. For instance, Zn(II)-lutidine amine (Zn-DPA) is a structural unit widely used in the construction of fluorescent sensors to detect phosphate groups using the strong binding ability of lutidine with phosphate groups [6]. In addition, owing to their binding affinity toward the triphosphate group of ATP, many metal ions such as Zn2+ [7], Cu2+ [8], Fe3+ [9], Cr3+ [10], Ga3+ [11] and Eu3+ [12], have been used to design fluorescent probes for ATP. However, reported fluorescence probes are usually prepared by organic synthesis, and the synthesis and purification processes are complicated and toxic. Therefore, it is necessary to develop abiotic fluorescence probes that can be easily prepared.

Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs), a subclass of MOFs, have been widely used because of their good biocompatibility, high water stability, and chemical stability [13]. ZIFs have been reported to encapsulate proteins [14], small biomolecules [15], fluorescent probes [16] and nanoparticle [17], making them widely used in sensing [18], energy storage [19], catalysis [20], gas adsorption [21], biomedicine [22] and other fields [23]. ZIF-90, synthesized using Zn2+ and imidazolate-2-carboxyaldehyde (2-ICA), has been extensively applied in biological sensing and drug delivery [24,25]. ZIF-90 has excellent ATP-selective responsive characteristics because the coordination between the phosphate and adenosine groups in ATP and Zn2+ is stronger than that of the imidazole group in 2-ICA, which ultimately lead to ZIF-90 structural collapse [15].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have received extensive attention in recent years owing to their remarkable properties, such as high biocompatibility, low toxicity, good water solubility, easy synthesis, and easy modification. Owing to the widespread existence of carbon in organisms, the green synthesis of CDs has become a widely discussed research topic. Numerous types of biomass-based carbon sources, including plant waste and animal waste, have been used to synthesize CDs through hydrothermal carbonization, microwave irradiation, and ultrasonic treatment [26–28]. These synthesized CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties such as aggregation-induced emission, aggregation-induced quenching, and ion-selective fluorescence response, which made them exceptionally flexible as signal markers in the construction of biosensors [29–34].

In this study, we propose a novel abiotic CDs-encapsulated ZIF-90 (CDs@ZIF-90) fluorescent probe for rapid ATP detection. The culture medium of Desulfovibrio sp., containing yeast powder and tryptone in seawater, was used as a raw reactant to prepare CDs that exhibited good water solubility, biocompatibility, and excellent fluorescence properties. Subsequently, the CDs@ZIF-90 probe was harvested with a rapid and straightforward one-pot reaction via encapsulation of the CDs into the ZIF-90 structure, rather than the commonly used post-modification method via absorption of CDs on ZIF-90, which usually requires a long reaction time owing to the low adsorption efficiency. However, our method only requires 1 h of reaction time [13,35]. The encapsulation of the CDs inhibited the fluorescence emission of the as-prepared CDs@ZIF-90. While ZIF-90 was used as the abiotic recognition material towards ATP, the presence of ATP could destroy the structural integrity of ZIF-90 via strong coordination between ATP and Zn2+. Thus, encapsulated CDs were released, leading to an increase in fluorescence intensity (Scheme 1 ). The synthesis of CDs and CDs@ZIF-90 is simple and avoids the complexity and toxicity associated with organic synthesis. Moreover, the fabricated biocompatible CDs@ZIF-90 exhibited a quick response with high specificity for ATP detection and excellent stability because no biological recognition materials, such as antibodies, lectins, and aptamers, were used. These advantages make the proposed CDs@ZIF-90 suitable for in-situ, non-destructive, and non-toxic detection of biological ATP.

Zinc acetate dihydrate, adenosine 5′-triphosphate disodium salt (ATP) and imidazole-2-carboxaldehyde (2-ICA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cytidine 5′ triphosphate disodium salt (CTP), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), and adenosine diphosphate (ADP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Yeast powder and human serum sample were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tryptone and dimethylformamide were purchased from Shanghai Heguo Information Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

FT-IR spectra were recorded by a Nicolet iS10 F T-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were examined by using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmittance electron microscopy (USA). The fluorescence spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CDs was performed on a Thermos Scientific Escalab Xi+ (USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the composites was performed on a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Japan). Zeta potential of CDs was determined by using a Malvern Zetasizer NANO ZS90 (UK).

CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.2 g yeast powder and 1.0 g tryptone were added to 100 mL sea water under stirring to obtain a fluid nutrient medium. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. After the reaction at 140 °C for 12 h, the solution was cooled to room temperature. After aging for 2 h, the acquired CDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (a cutting molecular weight of 3500) for 24 h. Finally, the CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

CDs@ZIF-90 was synthesized using a simple one-pot method. CDs solution (0.5 mL, 0.62 g L−1), DMF solution of 2-ICA (1 mL, 0.05 M), and a DMF solution of zinc acetate dihydrate (0.5 mL, 0.05 M) were mixed under vigorous stirring on a vortex mixing apparatus for 1 h. The obtained CDs@ZIF-90 was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 3 min and washed twice with DMF and DI water. Finally, CDs@ZIF-90 was dispersed in water and stored at 4 °C.

The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was calculated using the following equation [36]:
QYu = QYs (I u/I s) (A s/A u) (n u/n s) 2
Where QY depicts the fluorescence quantum yield, I represents the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the optical absorption, and n is the refractive index (1.33 for aqueous solution). The subscript “s” and “u” refer to the standards and sample, respectively. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QYs = 0.54) was chosen as the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of ATP with CDs@ZIF-90
ce:section.ce:para: The detection process is straightforward; 300 μL, 1.93 g L−1 CDs@ZIF-90 dispersion was mixed with 300 μL of ATP solutions of different concentrations. After incubation for 15 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded directly at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

To examine the selectivity of CDs@ZIF-90, CDs@ZIF-90 (300 μL, 1.93 g L−1) was incubated with ATP (300 μL, 20 μM) and interfering species (300 μL, 20 μM), including AMP, ADP, and CTP. After reacting for 15 min, the fluorescence responses of the mixtures were measured, and the changes in fluorescence intensity were extracted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of ATP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were diluted 20 times with DI water. ATP was then added to the diluent to prepare ATP solutions of different concentrations. Then, the CDs@ZIF-90 solution was mixed with the as-prepared human serum samples for 15 min to detect the fluorescence intensity.

Furthermore, the proposed CDs@ZIF-90 was applied to the rapid detection of ATP in bacterial biofilms. Biofilms of Desulfovibrio sp. were formed by anaerobic cultivation in Postgate medium for 3 days, with glass and stainless steel (316 L) as substrates. The biofilm on the solid surface was scraped off with a scalpel blade and dissolved in 5 mL of water, and then CDs@ZIF-90 was added for ATP detection.

The CDs were small regular spheres monodispersed in water with a diameter of approximately 3 nm (Fig. 1 A). The inert HRTEM image clearly shows the CDs with 0.21 nm wide lattice fringes corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite, indicating a graphite-like structure [37]. The DLS results indicated the particle size of the samples in solution, and the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs was estimated as 3.5 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the XPS C 1 s peak could be fitted with five binding energies: 284.7, 285.4, 285.9, 286.2, and 288.0 eV, which were attributed to the CC/C–C, C–S, C–N, C–O, and CN/CO bonds, respectively [38–40]. The surface chemical composition of the CDs was further verified by FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 2 C (red line), the peak centered at 3403 cm−1 suggested the presence of O–H bonds. The peaks at approximately 1633, 1403, 1133-1026 and 560 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO, CH3/ = C–N, SO, and S–S, respectively [41,42]. The XPS and FT-IR analyses indicated that the CDs were doped with nitrogen and sulfur, with functional groups coexisting on the CDs, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, ester, and sulfonic groups. These hydrophilic groups promote the dispersion ability and hydrophilicity of CDs in water.

As shown in Fig. S1, the as-prepared CDs exhibited interesting tunable emission properties in which the emission wavelengths depended on the excitation wavelengths. This excitation wavelength-dependent emission is a typical fluorescence property of CDs, and the underlying mechanisms have been extensively studied in the literature [43–45]. In this work, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs under excitation from 330 to 370 nm varied within a tolerable range, and the UV–vis spectrum of CDs exhibited wide and strong absorption in the ultraviolet region (Fig. 2D, black line). Hence, 360 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength in subsequent experiments, as is common for reported C-dots [46–48]. Additionally, the photostability of the prepared CDs was verified. Fig. S2 shows the fluorescence spectra of the CDs under UV light irradiation at a wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of the as-prepared CDs remained stable under UV irradiation for 0–120 min, indicating the remarkable photostability of the CDs at various irradiation times.

After encapsulating the CDs into the ZIF-90 framework, the morphology of the formed CDs@ZIF-90 is shown in Fig. 2A. CDs@ZIF-90 exhibited a smooth edge with a size of approximately 40 nm. Compared to the cubic structure of ZIF-90 without CDs encapsulation (Fig. S3), the elliptical boundary could be attributed to the adsorption of the CDs. The crystal structure of CDs@ZIF-90 was characterized using XRD. The obtained diffraction peaks of CDs@ZIF-90 (red line) are in good agreement with the simulated ZIF-90 (blue line) in a previous report [49]. Furthermore, compared to the XRD pattern of pure ZIF-90 without CDs encapsulation (black line), the fluctuating background noise in the XRD pattern of CDs@ZIF-90 indicated that the encapsulation of CDs reduced the crystallinity of ZIF-90 (Fig. 2B). The FT-IR spectrum of CDs@ZIF-90 (black line) displayed high similarity with that of ZIF-90 (blue line), where the band at 1668 cm−1 represented the aldehyde stretch of the imidazole ligands in ZIF-90 [50].

After the CDs were encapsulated in the ZIF-90 framework, the band at 1668 cm−1 became weaker, possibly due to the superimposed effect of CO at 1633 cm−1 [51] (Fig. 2C). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded to characterize the fabrication and detection processes. As shown in Fig. 2D, compared with CDs, which exhibited a wide and strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, the spectra of ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 were similar, with a sharp absorption peak at 288 nm, which could be attributed to the absorption of the imidazole group [52]. These results indicate that the ZIF-90 framework restrained the absorption signal of the CDs. After ATP was added to the CDs@ZIF-90 suspension, the absorption in the ultraviolet region increased, confirming the successful encapsulation of CDs into the CDs@ZIF-90 probe. The QYs of CDs and CDs@ ZIF-90 were calculated as 1.03% and 0.0053%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of ATP detection based on abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe
ce:section.ce:para: The cleavage response of ATP towards ZIF-90 has been reported and applied for selective ATP detection [53]. First, the morphological change of the CDs@ZIF-90 probe after ATP addition was characterized. Compared with the morphology of the CDs@ZIF-90 probe in the absence of ATP (Fig. 2A), the addition of ATP cleaved the structure of ZIF-90 due to the coordination of zinc ions and phosphate groups to form a metal chelate, leaving the irregular fragments observed (Fig. 3 A).

Subsequently, the change in fluorescence emission of the CDs@ZIF-90 probe before and after ATP addition was measured. As shown in Fig. 3B, compared to the fluorescence of the CDs@ZIF-90 probe in the absence of ATP (black line), the fluorescence emission of the CDs@ZIF-90 solution was enhanced after ATP addition (red line). Moreover, the effect of ZIF-90 on the fluorescence emission of the CDs@ZIF-90 was verified to demonstrate that the fluorescence enhancement of the CDs@ZIF-90 after ATP addition resulted from CDs release rather than ZIF-90 cleavage. The addition of ZIF-90 did not cause significant changes in the fluorescence emission of the CDs, and even a slight fluorescence enhancement was observed when a high concentration of ZIF-90 was added (Fig. S4). These results indicate that ZIF-90 had no inhibitory effect on the fluorescence emission of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ATP detection performances of the abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe
ce:section.ce:para: First, the working conditions for synthesizing the CDs were optimized to obtain high-quality CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs prepared by adjusting the experimental conditions was measured after aging for 2 h. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs synthesized with reactants from 2 g L−1 to 15 g L−1 decreased with increasing reactant concentration, which could be attributed to aggregation-caused quenching of the CDs (Fig. S5A).

Moreover, the effect of the reaction time on the preparation of the CDs was studied. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs increased with increasing reaction time and reached a plateau after 10 h of reaction (Fig. S5B). A reaction time of 12 h was selected for subsequent experiments. Furthermore, the effect of the reaction temperature on the preparation of CDs was explored in the range of 120–150 °C. The fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CDs reached a maximum at 140 °C (Fig. S5C); thus, this was chosen as the optimal reaction temperature.

Owing to the strong coordination between ATP and Zn2+, the proposed abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe exhibited a rapid response toward ATP detection. The concentration of CDs@ZIF-90 used for ATP detection was optimized to obtain the best analytical performance. The background signals of CDs@ZIF-90 under different concentrations are given as the gray parts of the columns, and the fluorescence changes of the CDs@ZIF-90 probes towards 80 μM ATP are shown in yellow or red columns (Fig. S6). Compared with the other concentrations, CDs@ZIF-90 (1.93 g L−1) held the best signal response capability. Fig. S7 shows the fluorescence responses of CDs@ZIF-90 after adding 80 μM ATP at different times. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 increased greatly within 30 s and gradually stabilized after 15 min with a remarkable fluorescence change. These results prove that the collapse of CDs@ZIF-90 under ATP stimuli was rapid and sensitive.

Next, the fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-90 were recorded at various ATP concentrations. In the concentration range of 2.5 μM–80 μM, with the increase in ATP concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 increased gradually (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4B displays the linear relationship between the changes in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@ZIF-90 nanoprobes (ΔF. I.), which were extracted by comparing the fluorescence signal before and after ATP addition, and ATP concentrations in the range from 2.5 μM to 15 μM and from 20 μM to 80 μM. In the concentration range of 2.5 μM–15 μM, the linear correlation followed the equation ΔF.I. = 65.15 × C ATP – 4.95. The detection limit was calculated as 0.38 μM. Meanwhile, in the range of 20 μM–80 μM, the linear correlation equation can be expressed as ΔF.I. = 8.77 × C ATP + 586.88. The selectivity of CDs@ZIF-90 for ATP detection was also investigated. The fluorescence intensity change caused by ATP was significantly higher than that caused by ADP, AMP, and CTP (Fig. 4C). These results demonstrated the high specificity of the abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe for ATP detection. Fig. 4D displays the stability of the CDs@ZIF-90 probe at various storage times within 12 d. Fluorescence emission of CDs@ZIF-90 and the corresponding changes caused by adding 20 μM ATP were stable with tolerable changes, indicating that the abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe had pronounced stability.

Compared with other previously reported sensors for ATP detection (Table 1 ), the proposed method is simple, straightforward, and possesses a wide dynamic range and low detection limit, which are superior or comparable to those of previously reported methods. In addition, abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 avoided using biological recognition materials, such as aptamers and antibodies, which endowed CDs@ZIF-90 with high stability for ATP detection.

Subsequently, the practical application of CDs@ZIF-90 in detecting ATP in complex matrices was evaluated. The standard recovery method was used to detect ATP in artificial human serum samples, and the results are presented in Table 2 . The experimental results showed that the recovery ratio of ATP in human serum samples was 102.82–104.61%, with an acceptable RSD of less than 7%. Besides, the accuracy of the proposed method was verified using a commercial ATP detection kit (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd, Beijing, China), and the accuracy was in the range of 91.91–129.13%. These results demonstrate that CDs@ZIF-90 has sufficient capability to detect ATP in real samples.

Finally, the abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe was applied to detect free ATP within biofilms, which is highly useful for studying bacterial metabolite status in biofilms. Glass and stainless steel sheets were suspended in a medium containing Desulfovibrio sp. and cultured in an anaerobic environment for 3 days. During this process, the bacteria attached to the solid surfaces, and a biofilm gradually formed on the solid surface. The concentration of free ATP within the biofilms was successfully measured; the on stainless steel was higher than that on glass substrates (Fig. 5 ). This is because stainless steel can provide electrons to Desulfovibrio sp., thus facilitating bacterial growth and adhesion processes.

In this study, a novel abiotic fluorescence probe (CDs@ZIF-90) was developed for ATP detection. CDs were prepared using a bacterial culture solution as the reactant, and CDs@ZIF-90 were synthesized by a straightforward one-pot method. Owing to the strong coordination between ATP and Zn2+, the CDs@ZIF-90 probe served as an abiotic recognition material towards ATP, which endowed CDs@ZIF-90 with high selectivity and a rapid response for ATP detection. At concentrations ranging from 2.5 μM to 80 μM, ATP could be sensitively detected within 15 min.

Furthermore, the fabrication and detection processes of the CDs@ZIF-90 probe avoided the use of biological recognition materials such as aptamers and antibodies, which made CDs@ZIF-90 highly stable for ATP detection, and the resulting changes in fluorescence intensity were remarkably steady for at least 12 days. Finally, the application feasibility of the abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe was confirmed in artificial human serum samples and then practically applied in the detection of ATP within biofilms. This paper presents a green synthesis route to prepare fluorescent CDs, which provides fresh insights into the resourceful treatment of bacterial cultivation waste. In addition, the proposed novel abiotic CDs@ZIF-90 probe, with the advantages of simple synthesis, rapid response, and high stability, holds significant promise for clinical and environmental analysis.

Yue Xin: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Dun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yan Zeng: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Peng Qi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 41876101 and 41906037), Shandong Provincial Marine Science and Technology Projects of 2018, Open fund of Wenzhou Key Laboratory of Sanitary Microbiology (Grant No. ZD202003KF01) and Nantong Scientific Plan Foundation (Grant No. JC2021054 and JC2020090).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2022.115021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adenosine 5′-triphosphate: an intracellular metabolic messenger

J. Am. Chem. Soc.
